truthparser — Parsing Boolean expressions¶

New in version 0.1.

Module author: Claude Gibert <claude.gibert@synopticview.co.uk>

The TruthParser class¶

class core.truthparser.TruthParser(dict)¶

The TruthParser analyses boolean expressions in the form: “a == 1 and b < 3 or c == 0”. If then builds a tree from the reverse polish notation of the expresion, loading each node and leaf with user-defined object instances. It is then able to “execute” the tree to let the user’s objects do their job.

The TruthParser uses pyparser Python module (http://pyparsing.wikispaces.com/) and handles comparison operator and logical operators: ==, !=, <, <=, >, >= together with and and or. It is quite simple and crude and could be improved greatly particularly for handling user’s syntax errors, but it does the job it was required to do. It can be improved later.

Some sort of abstraction has been put into developping the parser, it could be used for most applications which require truth parsing. The user of this class should implement a class with the following methods (what is the token parsed in the expression):

  • createLogical(self,what): returns an object instance to deal with x and y or x or y
  • createComparison(self,what) returns an object instance to deal with operator ==, !=, <, <=, >, >=
  • createVariable(self,what) returns an object instance to deal with variables defines in the expression, basically any string which is not a number or an operator
  • createValue(self,what) returns an object instance to deal with hardcoded floating point values which are part of the expression.

The TruthParser class provides default behaviour for nodes holding a logical (and, or), a comparison (<,>,== etc...) and a value (literal floating point value). Nothing can be supplied by default for Variable.

Once the tree representing the expression is built, it is executed to produce a result. The following object methods are called during the process:

  • Object created by createLogical: __call__(self,a,b) returns the result of the logical operation between a and b (or whatever they contain)
  • Object created by createComparison: __call__(self,a,b) returns the result of the comparison of a and b
  • Object created by createVariable: __call__(self) returns the value of the variable in the expression
  • Object created by createValue: __call__(self) returns the value.
parse(expression, creator)¶
Parameters:
  • expression (string) – the text expression to be parsed.
  • creator (an object instance with the necessary methods see introduction) – called with one parameter, the token found, should return an object instance to be stored in a node.

Parses the text, creates the expression tree and executes it calling the objects provided by the creator (please see introduction).

build(x, creator)¶

Builds the expression tree, called internally.

execute(tree)¶

Executes the tree by calling the object instances, called internally.

TruthParser Examples¶

Here is a simple example:

from semperpy.core.truthparser import TruthParser

class MyParser(object):

    def compile(self,expression):
        parser = TruthParser()
        return parser.parse(expression,self)

p = MyParser()
print p.compile('2 < 4 and (9 < 1 or 2 == 2')
# returns True

This is what the TruthParser can do by default, nice but it doesn’t take us very far. Let’s slighly improve it:

from semperpy.core.truthparser import TruthParser

class MyParser(object):

    def createVariable(self,what):
        return TruthParser.Value(self.variables_[what])

    def compile(self,expression,**variables):
        self.variables_ = variables
        parser = TruthParser()
        return parser.parse(expression,self)

p = MyParser()
print p.compile('z <= 4 and (x > 1 or y == 2)',x = 10.0, y = 20.0, z = 3.0) # True
print p.compile('z <= 4 and (x > 1 or y == 2)',x = 1.0, y = 2.0, z = 3.0)   # True
print p.compile('z <= 4 and (x > 1 or y == 2)',x = 1.0, y = 2.0, z = 5.0)   # False

This is slightly more useful if we know the expression in advance and the value of the variables at run time only. The TruthParser was designed for an application a little more sophisticated, the calculation of masks. This example shows how to compute a mask on arrays according to an expression:

import numpy as np
from semperpy.core.truthparser import TruthParser

class MyParser(object):

    def __init__(self):
        self.arrays_ = dict(
            x = np.arange(100),
            y = np.arange(99,-1,-1),
            z = np.random.rand(100) * 100,
        )

    def createVariable(self,what):
        return TruthParser.Value(self.arrays_[what])

    def createLogical(self,what):
        if what == 'and':
            return np.logical_and
        else:
            return np.logical_or

    def compile(self,expression):
        parser = TruthParser()
        return parser.parse(expression,self)

    def extract(self,mask):
        return self.arrays_['z'][mask]

p = MyParser()
mask = p.compile('x == 49 and y == 50')
print mask
mask = p.compile('x < y')
print mask
value = p.extract(mask)
print value.shape
print value

The program produces the output:

[False False False False False False False False False False False False
 False False False False False False False False False False False False
 False False False False False False False False False False False False
 False False False False False False False False False False False False
 False  True False False False False False False False False False False <-
 False False False False False False False False False False False False
 False False False False False False False False False False False False
 False False False False False False False False False False False False
 False False False False]
[ True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True <-
  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True <-
  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True <-
  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True  True <-
  True  True False False False False False False False False False False <-
 False False False False False False False False False False False False
 False False False False False False False False False False False False
 False False False False False False False False False False False False
 False False False False]
(50,)
[ 74.81871765  51.32676716  80.04210581  44.91719832  91.08454225
  79.85584726  41.72192775  83.58348206  15.76761108  28.59978859
  62.13575457  60.91221397  50.9117611   24.52117651  83.9582438
  66.06572606  14.7983316   62.25920367  10.28110361  35.65820036
  32.09910369  38.49067582  24.58884459   5.61183983  73.55411382
  11.84061137  78.59969057  94.35803136  53.61091119  55.52293499
  15.16219035  10.34310616  42.76413067  32.72124625  91.36094832
   9.59616351  14.48611709  72.42349225  90.82700306  10.03005652
  45.08080076  11.23162564  82.12659091   5.65627268  95.54599445
  36.45911579  32.76919863   7.9673354   33.95351563  23.11777119]

A full working example is implemented in :class: observations.odsreader using netcdf files. Masks are calculated on arrays containing metadata, the mask is then used to read selected observations.
